ops.body: Stage A parity re-score complete (artifact /private/tmp/cc-steer-parity-stageA/stageA_results.json, sha256 c96cf72e…; 20 compute_logprobs calls, checkpoint addressed via list_checkpoints — not guessed; zero training calls). VERDICT: broad multi-row drift, not an outlier. Per-variant max/count>0.05: current-bf16 0.2603 (6/20), current-f32 0.2441 (9/20), rebuilt-bf16 0.2037 (8/20), rebuilt-f32 0.2202 (8/20). Neither f32 normalization nor an mlx-lm 0.31.3 base rebuild closes the gate; failures concentrate on mid-probability rows (0.24–0.93) while near-0 rows match to 1e-4 — the 4-bit base's quantization genuinely shifts mid-confidence predictions. DECISION (first-principles, per the clean-slate directive): the watcher promotion gate becomes EVAL-WHAT-YOU-SERVE — after convert_peft_to_mlx, score the full frozen frame through the actual served 4-bit MLX artifact locally (f32 normalization) and feed the promotion gate (AUC floor, coverage sign-test, threshold, write_probs) from those local probs. Tinker keeps training + per-checkpoint selection. The parity check dies as a gate (a conversion bug now surfaces directly as served AUC at chance → rejected by the existing floor) and is replaced by a persisted 20-row Tinker-vs-served diagnostic (per-row diffs journaled — closing the observability hole that forced this re-score). 8-bit serving rejected: doubles daemon RAM yet still measures a model we don't serve. Tolerance raise stays rejected. Implementation = T3.1 on the cc-steer tree (opus xhigh), validation includes scoring the rejected run's existing converted artifact over the full frame BEFORE any new Tinker spend — that number tells us whether this candidate would honestly promote under the new gate.
